back: Goal: translate yesterday's pub conversation into precise TypeScript types. By the end of this lesson you'll have written lib/types.ts — the single most important file in the codebase, even though it doesn't render anything.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-01-foundations/02-data-modeling.md: 'For each round, the i-th element of team.r1 (or team.r2, qf,...'
back: For each round, the i-th element of team.r1 (or team.r2, qf, sf) corresponds to the i-th element of ROUND1MATCHES (or ROUND2MATCHES, QFMATCHES, SFMATCHES).

front: In react-snooker/chapter-01-foundations/02-data-modeling.md: Why winner? is optional?
back: This is the fork in the road that powers the whole app. Re-read Lesson 1's scoring rules: a match that hasn't been played has null (or, equivalently here, undefined) for every team's score. The data-level mechanism that produces that behaviour is the absence of match.winner.

front: In react-snooker/chapter-01-foundations/02-data-modeling.md: Why score? and seed1? are optional?
back: score is optional because finished matches have one and pending matches don't. Same logic as winner.

front: In react-snooker/chapter-01-foundations/02-data-modeling.md: 1. r1, r2, qf, sf are arrays of strings — but final is just a string?
back: Lesson 1 reminded us there's only one Final match. So a team only makes one Final pick. We could have written final: string[] with one element, but that would cost us readability everywhere we touch it: team.final[0] === 'Wu Yize' reads worse than team.final === 'Wu Yize'. So we accept the asymmetry. The cost is that scoring code has one branch for Final and another for everything else (we'll see this in Lesson 3).

front: In react-snooker/chapter-01-foundations/02-data-modeling.md: 2. The cosmetics live next to the picks?
back: color, accent, icon, motto are decorative. They drive every gradient, badge, and chip in the UI. They live on the same object as the picks because each team is one logical thing — not two (a "team identity" plus "team picks"). Splitting them would buy nothing and add a level of indirection.

front: In react-snooker/chapter-01-foundations/02-data-modeling.md: 3. r1Players? and r2Players? exist for a subtle scoring case?
back: These aren't needed for normal scoring — they're a hint used by calculateTeamScores when a team's pick advances through the round in a non-obvious way (e.g. the team picked someone in their R1 spot, but the R2 spot is for a different bracket position than they expected). For most teams these arrays are unused. They're optional precisely because most teams won't have them.

front: In react-snooker/chapter-01-foundations/02-data-modeling.md: Answer to #1 (because it's a real gotcha)?
back: winner?: string and winner: string | undefined look almost the same, but they differ on whether the field has to be specified at all when constructing the object.

back: Goal: write lib/scoring.ts — two pure functions, no React, no JSX, no DOM. By the end of this lesson the heart of the application is beating, and you haven't typed import React even once.

back: Interview tip. When asked "how do you structure a React app?", the seniors say "separate pure data/logic from rendering." If you can point to your own lib/ folder full of pure functions and your components/ folder of pure presentation, you've got a working answer with proof.

back: If the match isn't finished, return null. Otherwise, return 3 if the pick won, 1 otherwise.

back: This is the "pending" branch. Two ways the function arrives here:

front: In react-snooker/chapter-01-foundations/03-pure-functions-and-scoring.md: Branch 2: if (pick === match.winner) return 3;?
back: If we reach this line, we know match.winner is a string (TypeScript narrowed it from the previous early-return). If the team's pick matches the winner, they get 3 points.

back: The match has a winner, but the pick wasn't them. The team gets a consolation point. Snooker's hard; you tried.

front: In react-snooker/chapter-01-foundations/03-pure-functions-and-scoring.md: The whole function in one breath?
back: > "If the match isn't finished, return null. Otherwise, return 3 if the pick won, 1 otherwise."

back: ``ts const r1Details: ScoreDetail[] = team.r1.map((pick, i) => { const pts = scorePick(pick, ROUND1MATCHES[i]); r1 += pts || 0; return { match: ROUND1MATCHES[i], pick, points: pts, correct: pts === null ? null : pts === 3, }; }); ``

back: ``ts const finalMatch = FINAL_MATCH[0]; const fPts = scorePick(team.final, finalMatch); f += fPts || 0; const fDetails: ScoreDetail[] = [{ match: finalMatch, pick: team.final, points: fPts, correct: fPts === null ? null : fPts === 3, }]; ``

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/01-why-nextjs-typescript.md: 'Goal: understand the framework decision tree well enough to ...'
back: Goal: understand the framework decision tree well enough to defend "we used Next.js" in an interview without looking nervous. Beginners pick frameworks by vibe; seniors pick by requirements.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/01-why-nextjs-typescript.md: 'Interview answer template:* "Compile-time errors, self-docum...'
back: Interview answer template:* "Compile-time errors, self-documenting types, and editor support. The cost is a small upfront learning curve and slightly more verbose code, but for any project bigger than a 50-line script the win is overwhelming.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/01-why-nextjs-typescript.md: 'I'm building a single-page React app for a fantasy sports le...'
back: I'm building a single-page React app for a fantasy sports league. ~250 hand-typed data records, 5 tabs, no backend, no auth, no real-time, deployment to Vercel. Should I use Next.js or Vite + React? Lay out the tradeoffs honestly. Don't just say 'use the popular one.'

front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/01-why-nextjs-typescript.md: Vite wins on:?
back: - Dev server startup speed. Vite boots in ~200ms; Next takes 2-3 seconds. - Less framework magic. What you see in your code is what runs. - Smaller bundle by default. No SSR overhead, no Next runtime. - Easier to migrate to a non-React framework later. Vite is generic.

front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/01-why-nextjs-typescript.md: Next.js wins on:?
back: - Vercel deployment with literally one click. Push to GitHub, import on Vercel, done. Vite needs a hosting target you configure (Netlify, Cloudflare Pages, etc.). - TypeScript + Tailwind + ESLint pre-wired. npx create-next-app gives you a working project with all of these. With Vite you assemble them yourself. - Image and font optimization out of the box (next/font, next/image). - Routing if you ever expand. If next year you add a /leaderboard/[year] route, Next has it free; Vite needs react-router-dom. - Server components for free. Even though we won't use them heavily, the option is there.

front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/01-why-nextjs-typescript.md: 2. Self-documenting code?
back: When you write function calculateTeamScores(team: Team): TeamScores, anyone reading the code knows the input shape, the output shape, and they can Cmd+Click to navigate to either. Types are documentation that the compiler keeps honest. Comments drift. Types don't.

front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/01-why-nextjs-typescript.md: 3. Editor superpowers?
back: VS Code, Cursor, Windsurf — every modern editor turns into a god-tier autocomplete machine when you have types. You type team. and it shows you r1, r2, qf, sf, final, .... You don't have to memorize the shape; the editor reminds you.

front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/01-why-nextjs-typescript.md: When NOT to use TypeScript?
back: There's a reasonable counterargument for tiny scripts (under 50 lines), prototypes you'll throw away, and learning exercises where the type ceremony obscures the lesson. For anything shipped to production, multi-file, or maintained by more than one person — TypeScript wins.

back: Some Next plugins assume Tailwind is present. Having it costs ~5KB of CSS and zero developer time.

back: Why does npm run dev need run but npm start doesn't? Historical: a few script names (start, test, install, restart, stop) are built-in shortcuts; the rest need run. It's a wart. Live with it.

front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/02-scaffolding-the-project.md: "name", "version", "private"?
back: - "name" is the project name. Used by tools, never by your code. - "version" follows semver (semantic versioning). Doesn't matter for an unpublished app; matters a lot for npm packages. - "private": true" is a safety latch. It tells npm "do not publish this to the public registry." Don't omit it. Many beginners have accidentally published their unfinished apps because they didn't have this.

front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/03-creating-data-files.md: 1. Real apps spend a lot of time on data plumbing?
back: In an interview, you'll be asked to build a feature in 45 minutes. A third of that time is going to be typing fixture data so you have something to render. Practicing it on a real, larger-than-toy dataset prepares you for that.

front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/03-creating-data-files.md: 2. Typing the data forces you to feel the invariants?
back: When you write a Team object and have to lay out its 16 R1 picks in the same order as the 16 matches in ROUND1_MATCHES — you internalize Lesson 1.2's invariant in your fingers. That's worth more than reading about it three times.

front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/03-creating-data-files.md: export const ROUND1_MATCHES: Match[]?
back: - export so other files (lib/scoring.ts, components) can import it. - const because we never reassign the array. (We could mutate the array's contents, but TypeScript's readonly modifiers can prevent that too — we don't bother for an internal data file.) - : Match[] is the type annotation: an array of Match objects. TypeScript will complain if you forget a required field on any item, or add a typo, or pass a number where a string is expected. That's the whole point.

front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/03-creating-data-files.md: The objects themselves?
back: ``ts { id: 1, p1: 'Zhao Xintong', p2: 'Liam Highfield', winner: 'Zhao Xintong', score: '10-7', seed1: 1 }, ``

front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/03-creating-data-files.md: Pending matches look different?
back: By the end of the tournament every match has a winner. But during the tournament, before any QF matches were finished, the QF_MATCHES array would have looked like this:

front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/03-creating-data-files.md: The four other rounds?
back: ``ts export const ROUND2MATCHES: Match[] = [ /* 8 entries */ ]; export const QFMATCHES: Match[] = [ / 4 entries / ]; export const SFMATCHES: Match[] = [ /* 2 entries */ ]; export const FINALMATCH: Match[] = [ / 1 entry / ]; ``

front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/03-creating-data-files.md: How the file ends?
back: The full file is about 90 lines. It exports five constants. The order is R1 first, Final last — chronological order from the user's point of view, not bracket-evaluation order. The reader's brain is happier in chronological order.

front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/03-creating-data-files.md: What's in each team?
back: - Identity: name, color, accent, icon, motto. The color and accent are the team's primary brand pair, used for gradients in headers and badges. - Picks: r1 (16 strings), r2 (8 strings), qf (4 strings), sf (2 strings), final (1 string).

front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/03-creating-data-files.md: How to type 16 picks without going insane?
back: Realistically you do this with a printout of the bracket on one side and your editor on the other. For each match in ROUND1_MATCHES, ask the team owner "who do you pick?" and write the name in the corresponding slot.

front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/03-creating-data-files.md: Color choices?
back: Each team has a primary color and an accent. The convention used in this codebase:

front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/03-creating-data-files.md: Two optional fields you might see?
back: Recall from Lesson 1.2 that Team has two optional helper arrays: r1Players? and r2Players?. You'll see them on a few teams. They're only used in edge cases for scoring. Most of the eight teams will not have these arrays. Don't worry about them yet; you'll meet them again in Chapter 4 if at all.

front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/03-creating-data-files.md: Record<string, PlayerInfo> explained?
back: Record<K, V> is TypeScript shorthand for "an object whose keys are of type K and values are of type V." Record<string, PlayerInfo> means "any string key is allowed; every value must be a PlayerInfo."

back: Yes, you can put flag emojis directly in TypeScript strings. UTF-8 encoding handles them natively. Just paste them in:

back: ``ts 'Liam Highfield': { country: '🏴', status: 'Qualifier', flag: 'ENG' }, // no seed ``

front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/03-creating-data-files.md: Why a dictionary, not an array?
back: Already covered in Lesson 1.2. The most common operation is "given a player's name, get their metadata" — that's an O(1) hash lookup with a dictionary, or an O(n) .find() with an array. The dictionary wins on both performance and readability.

back: Interview question: "What does the key prop do?" The wrong answer is "it makes the warning go away." The right answer is "it tells React the identity of each list item across renders, so React can reconcile the DOM correctly when the list changes."

That means it gets shipped to the browser as JavaScript and can use useState, useEffect, event handlers, etc.

front: In react-snooker/chapter-03-first-component/02-rendering-the-standings.md: What is React doing??
back: When the data changes — say, a team's score goes up and the order shifts — React needs to figure out which DOM nodes to keep, which to update, and which to throw away. Without keys, React falls back to comparing by position in the array: the first row is the first row, the second is the second, etc.

front: In react-snooker/chapter-03-first-component/02-rendering-the-standings.md: The classic key bug?
back: Beginners often write key={i} because the index is right there. It works right up until the list reorders or has an item inserted in the middle, at which point React applies updates to the wrong rows and you get bizarre bugs (form inputs lose their values, animations attach to the wrong row, etc.).

back: Senior rule: if a piece of data can be derived from existing state or props, don't put it in state. Compute it on every render. Storing it duplicates the source of truth and creates synchronization bugs. State is for the minimum set of changeable values.

front: In react-snooker/chapter-04-state-and-hooks/01-useState-mental-model.md: The mental loop?
back: `` 1. React calls your function (render). 2. Your function returns JSX. 3. React updates the DOM to match the JSX. 4. User does something (clicks, types). 5. Event handler fires; calls setSomething(...). 6. React queues a re-render. 7. Go to 1. ``

front: In react-snooker/chapter-04-state-and-hooks/01-useState-mental-model.md: Rule 1: Call hooks at the top level. Never inside loops, conditions, or nested functions?

front: In react-snooker/chapter-04-state-and-hooks/01-useState-mental-model.md: Rule 2: Call hooks only from React function components or other hooks?
back: You can call useState inside a function component (function Foo()) or inside a custom hook (function useFoo()). Not inside a regular utility function. The reason is the same — React's per-component tracker only runs during component rendering.

front: In react-snooker/chapter-04-state-and-hooks/01-useState-mental-model.md: Hooks are detectable by name?
back: The convention: hooks start with use. useState, useEffect, useMemo, useReducer, useRef, useContext, etc. Custom hooks must also start with use — that's how the linter knows to enforce the rules on them.

front: In react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/03-pivots-and-internal-state.md: When to keep state local?
back: - Only this component reads it. - Only this component writes it. - The state doesn't need to survive remounting. - The state doesn't need to be shared with the URL or persistence.

front: In react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/03-pivots-and-internal-state.md: When to lift state up?
back: - A sibling component needs to read it. - A parent component needs to react to it. - The state should persist (e.g., URL param, localStorage). - Two pieces of state must stay in sync.

front: In react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/03-pivots-and-internal-state.md: The diff is roughly:?
back: | Concern | Local state | Lifted state | | ------- | ----------- | ------------ | | Component count needing it | 1 | 2+ | | Persistence required | No | Sometimes | | URL reflects it | No | Sometimes | | Resets on remount | OK | Often not OK | | Test isolation | Easier | Harder |

front: In react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/03-pivots-and-internal-state.md: 11.3. Lifting active into the orchestrator "just in case"?

back: The features work. Now make the app robust. This lesson is the bag of small, high-leverage improvements that separate "demo-quality" from "ship-quality."

front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/01-production-polish.md: 2.1. Use semantic HTML, not <div> soup?
back: A <button> is not a <div onClick={...}>. The browser gives you keyboard support, focus rings, and screen-reader announcements for free when you use the right element.

front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/01-production-polish.md: 2.2. Every image must have alt text?
back: We don't use heavily — most icons come from lucide-react (which renders SVG with aria-hidden by default). But if you ever add , write alt="Trophy" next to it. Decorative images get alt="" (empty string, not missing).

front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/01-production-polish.md: 2.3. Tab interfaces need role="tablist", role="tab", role="tabpanel"?
back: Open components/SnookerFantasyLeague.tsx. Find the tab nav. Right now it looks like:

front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/01-production-polish.md: 2.4. Every interactive element must be keyboard-reachable?
back: Try this: open your app in a browser and press Tab repeatedly. Can you get to every button? Every link? Every team row in the standings?

front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/01-production-polish.md: 2.5. Color contrast: text must be readable?
back: Open Chrome DevTools → Lighthouse → Accessibility audit. It will flag any text whose contrast ratio drops below 4.5:1 (3:1 for large text).

front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/01-production-polish.md: 3.1. The class-component reality?
back: Error boundaries are one of the two places React still requires a class component (the other is the componentDidCatch lifecycle that error boundaries themselves use). There is no hook for this. Yet.

front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/01-production-polish.md: 3.3. The interview question this answers?
back: > Q: "How do you handle errors in a React app?" > > A: "Three layers. (1) Error boundaries around any component subtree that could crash, with a friendly fallback UI and a logger that ships the error to Sentry. (2) Try/catch around any async work that could reject — fetch failures, JSON parse errors. (3) Defensive null checks in the render path for any data that might be missing. Error boundaries don't catch errors in event handlers or async code, so the three layers complement each other."

front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/01-production-polish.md: 4.1. When does this fire??
back: Whenever a route's server component is doing async work (await, dynamic imports). Right now our app is fully static — all data is in lib/. So loading.tsx will rarely fire in this app.

front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/01-production-polish.md: 4.2. Why aria-live="polite"??
back: Screen readers announce the loading state to the user, but in a non-disruptive way. aria-live="assertive" interrupts whatever the user is hearing — too aggressive for a spinner.

front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/01-production-polish.md: 5.1. What to look at?
back: - Size — the JS for just this route's code. - First Load JS — the JS the browser downloads when it first hits this route, including shared chunks. - Shared chunks — code split out so that navigating between routes doesn't re-download it.

front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/01-production-polish.md: 5.2. The numbers to worry about?
back: For a public-facing app, First Load JS under 200 KB is excellent. Under 500 KB is fine. Over 1 MB is a red flag.

back: Code that isn't deployed isn't real, and code that isn't tested isn't trusted. This lesson teaches you to ship the app to the public internet and to write the small set of tests that catch the bugs that actually happen.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/02-deployment-and-testing.md: 'Per the project rules, deployment goes through GitHub → Verc...'
back: Per the project rules, deployment goes through GitHub → Vercel automatically. We are not using vercel deploy from the CLI. We are not using Netlify. We push to GitHub; Vercel pulls from GitHub.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/02-deployment-and-testing.md: 'The more your tests resemble the way your software is used, ...'
back: The more your tests resemble the way your software is used, the more confidence they can give you. — Kent C. Dodds

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/02-deployment-and-testing.md: 'A small project deserves a small test suite. Don't aim for 1...'
back: A small project deserves a small test suite. Don't aim for 100% coverage; aim for the five tests that catch the bugs that actually happen.

front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/02-deployment-and-testing.md: 4.1. Local .env.local?
back: Create .env.local in the project root (it's already gitignored):

front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/02-deployment-and-testing.md: 4.2. Vercel environment variables?
back: In the Vercel dashboard: Project → Settings → Environment Variables. Add the same keys, but with the production values. Choose which environments they apply to: Production, Preview, Development.

front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/02-deployment-and-testing.md: What's worth noting?
back: - We test the pure functions because they're cheap and high-confidence. Pure-function tests are the highest ROI tests in any codebase. - We test the shape of the return value, not specific scores from real data. Tying tests to real-world results is brittle — when the tournament resets next year, your tests break for the wrong reason. - We test boundary conditions: empty arrays, undefined winners. Bugs hide in the boundaries.

front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/02-deployment-and-testing.md: What this test catches?
back: - Hydration errors — if the server-rendered HTML doesn't match the client tree, Playwright would see a console error. - Routing bugs — if a tab click stops working, the test fails. - Missing accessibility roles — getByRole('tab') only finds elements with the right ARIA. Half of writing E2E tests is being forced to add proper a11y, which is a feature.

front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/02-deployment-and-testing.md: Why include npm run build??
back: Because a passing test suite isn't worth much if the production build is broken. The build is itself a giant smoke test — it type-checks, bundles, and lints everything.

front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/02-deployment-and-testing.md: Why not run E2E in CI yet??
back: Playwright in CI requires a Linux runner with browsers preinstalled, plus more orchestration. It's worth doing eventually. For a personal portfolio project, run E2E locally before pushing big changes.

front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 1.3. The variant for non-technical interviewers?
back: If you're talking to a recruiter or a non-engineer hiring manager:

front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 2.1. "Walk me through your folder structure."?
back: > "Top-level: app/ for routes, components/ for UI, lib/ for data and pure logic, course/ for documentation. Inside components/, I split by role, not by type: a tabs/ folder for top-level tab components, a standings/ folder for everything that's only used inside the standings tab, and so on. The advantage is that when I want to delete the analytics tab, I delete one folder and don't have to chase down stragglers in a generic widgets/ directory."

front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 2.2. "How does state management work in your app?"?
back: > "I deliberately don't use Redux or Zustand. The app has two pieces of shared state — the active tab and the selected team — and both live in the top-level SnookerFantasyLeague component as plain useState. Tab-local state, like which round is selected inside the matches tab, lives inside that tab. The rule I follow: lift state only when something else needs it. State libraries are tools for the day a useState tree becomes painful — and that day hasn't come for an 8-team app."

front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 2.3. "How do you decide when to extract a component?"?
back: > "Two triggers. First, when I copy-paste the same JSX twice — the second time I write a function. Second, when a piece of JSX has a name: 'this is a stat card,' 'this is a path step.' Naming forces extraction. I avoid premature extraction because over-componentized React is just as painful as under-componentized — you end up chasing props through ten files instead of two."

front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 2.4. "How do you handle data fetching?"?
back: > "Right now this app's data is hardcoded — eight teams, ~60 matches, all in lib/. So technically there's no fetching. The way I'd add it: convert app/page.tsx to an async server component, await fetch from a sports-results API at request time, and pass the data down. The pure scoring functions in lib/scoring.ts would not change at all. That's the value of separating data from logic from UI."

front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 2.5. "What's the trade-off between server and client components?"?
back: > "Server components run only on the server. They can read environment variables and talk to databases, but they can't use useState or event handlers. Client components run in the browser. They have hooks and interactivity, but they bloat the JS bundle. The default in App Router is server, and I push 'use client' down to leaves — only the orchestrator and any tab that uses useState get that directive. The static parts above stay server-rendered."

front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 2.6. "How do you optimize performance in React?"?
back: > "Three layers. First, the render layer — useMemo for derived data, React.memo for expensive components, but only after profiling says I need them. Second, the bundle layer — next/dynamic for code-splitting heavy tabs like analytics with Recharts. Third, the paint layer — loading.tsx for instant skeletons during transitions. I never optimize before measuring; the React DevTools Profiler is my first stop."

front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 2.7. "How do you handle errors?"?
back: > "An error boundary at the root catches anything thrown during render and shows a friendly fallback. Async errors don't bubble through error boundaries, so I wrap fetches in try/catch and surface the message in the UI. In production I'd ship componentDidCatch output to Sentry. And I lean hard on TypeScript to make 'this object might be undefined' a compile-time error rather than a runtime one."

front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 2.8. "What's your testing strategy?"?
back: > "Five tests for this app. Three Vitest unit tests on the pure scoring functions — those are the highest ROI tests in the codebase. One React Testing Library integration test that renders the standings tab and asserts every team name is in the DOM. One Playwright E2E that confirms tab switching works. Plus the full TypeScript build, which is itself a giant static test. I aim for confidence, not coverage percentage."

front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 2.9. "How would you scale this to 1,000 teams?"?
back: > "Three things change. First, the data moves out of lib/ into a database — Postgres for relational picks-vs-matches, with a real foreign key instead of array-index correspondence. Second, the orchestrator's useMemo over all teams becomes a paginated server-side query — you don't render 1,000 rows in a table. Third, the Recharts visualizations either get aggregated server-side or switch to a canvas-based library because SVG can't draw 1,000 bars. None of those changes touch the UI layer's component shape — that's a deliberate result of the data/logic/UI separation."

front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 2.10. "What would you do differently?"?
back: This is the one that trips most candidates. They say "nothing." Wrong answer. The right answer:

front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 3.10. The testing prompt?
back: > "Write [3 unit + 1 integration + 1 E2E] tests. Unit tests cover [PURE FUNCTION] with [3 BOUNDARY CASES]. Integration test renders [COMPONENT] with [REAL DATA] and asserts [VISIBLE BEHAVIOR]. E2E test opens the app and [USER ACTION]. Use Vitest, RTL, Playwright."

front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 5.2. "Why didn't you use Redux / Zustand / TanStack Query?"?
back: > "Because the app has two pieces of shared state and zero remote data. Redux solves a problem this app doesn't have yet. TanStack Query solves a problem this app will have the day I add live API data — and that's the day I'd pull it in. Premature library adoption is a cost: extra learning, extra surface area for bugs, extra bundle size. I add libraries when local code starts to suffer, not preemptively."

front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 5.3. "How would I know if a feature you added broke something?"?
back: > "The CI pipeline runs lint, type-check, unit tests, integration tests, and the production build on every PR. The Playwright E2E covers the tab-switching happy path. Vercel preview deployments give a clickable URL on every PR for visual review. Beyond that, I'd want a real user-tracking and error-tracking layer in production — Sentry for crashes, PostHog or similar for usage. None of that is in the project yet because it's a personal app, but I'd add it on day one of a paid project."

front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 5.4. "What's the most React-specific bug you've fixed?"?
back: Tell a story. The structure is STAR: Situation, Task, Action, Result.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/README.md: 'You can build it. Now learn to ship it and talk about it. Th...'
back: You can build it. Now learn to ship it and talk about it. This chapter is the difference between a junior developer who finished a tutorial and an engineer who can stand behind their work in an interview.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/README.md: 'A structured, beginner-friendly course that teaches you Reac...'
back: A structured, beginner-friendly course that teaches you React from scratch by rebuilding a real, working fantasy-sports app — one component at a time, in the order a senior engineer would actually build it. Designed to take you from zero to interview-ready.

front: In react-snooker/README.md: Pace yourself?
back: The full course is about 35,000 words plus typing the code. Realistically:

front: In react-snooker/README.md: Don't skip Chapter 1?
back: Chapter 1 has zero React in it. It's about the problem and the data model. Beginners always want to skip to "the React part." Don't. Half of senior interview questions are "How would you model X?" and "What state is owned where?" — and those are answered before any JSX gets typed. Chapter 1 is where you build the muscle for that.

back: A nine-lesson walk-back from a finished 1,654-line React app to the first commit it would have come from. Read alongside _source/SnookerFantasyLeague.jsx open in another window.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: 'For each round, the i-th element of team.r1 (or r2, or qf, o...'
back: For each round, the i-th element of team.r1 (or r2, or qf, or sf) corresponds to the i-th element of ROUND1MATCHES (or ROUND2MATCHES, …).

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: 'State up, callbacks down. The state lives at the highest com...'
back: State up, callbacks down. The state lives at the highest component that needs to see all of it. The leaf components don't own state; they receive props and call back when they want a change.

front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: 'One feature. One file (or a small set of named files). One s...'
back: One feature. One file (or a small set of named files). One sentence per change. Avoid technology names. Avoid superlatives. Mention specific data fields and component names. Refer to behavior, not "polish."

front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: Imagine the pub conversation?
back: You and seven mates watch the World Snooker Championship every May. Sheffield, Crucible, two weeks of green-baize warfare. Someone — probably you — says: "We should make a fantasy league out of this." Everyone agrees. Pints are raised. Now what?

front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: The 8 teams?
back: There are eight fantasy teams: Invincibles, Uncredibles, Hopeless, Clueless, Break Builders United, The Untouchables, Selbies, One Four Sevens. Each one is a person (or a couple) sitting at home with their bracket, picking who they think wins each match. Each team needs:

front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: The scoring rules (3 bullets)?
back: - 3 points if your pick wins the match. - 1 point (a "consolation") if your pick loses — you still showed up. - null / pending if the match hasn't been played yet — neither right nor wrong, just not scored.

front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: What the app needs to do?
back: When the tournament is live, you want to be able to: see who's leading, see how each round shook out, drill into one team's picks, drill into one player's appearances, and see charts of who agreed and who didn't. That's it. No login, no database, no sharing. It's a static page; the data is edited by hand as results come in.

front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: Vibe prompt you would have used?
back: > "I want to build a single-page React app for a fantasy snooker league. Eight teams pick winners across five rounds (R1: 16 picks, R2: 8, QF: 4, SF: 2, Final: 1). Scoring: 3 points if the pick wins, 1 if it loses, null while the match is unplayed. No database — the bracket and team picks are edited by hand in code. The page should have tabs for Standings, Matches, Predictions, Players, and Analytics. Don't write any code yet — just confirm you understand the problem and ask me anything that's ambiguous."

front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: Why not put selectedTeam inside <PredictionsTab>??
back: Because then the <StandingsTab>'s row click couldn't reach it. The state has to live at the lowest common ancestor of every component that needs to read or write it. For selectedTeam, the readers are <PredictionsTab> and the writers are both <StandingsTab> (when you click a row) and <PredictionsTab> (when you click a team chip inside it). Their lowest common ancestor is <SnookerFantasyLeague> itself, so that's where the state lives.

back: The most interesting chart on the page is <LeagueAgreementChart>. The question it answers is: "For each match in this round, of the 8 teams, how many picked the actual winner?" That's a pivot — the source data is per-team-per-round, but the chart needs per-match-per-round.

front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: Milestone 1 — "Just give me the standings"?
back: Pre-tournament. R1 hasn't started. You have the bracket. You have eight friends' picks. You want a single screen that shows team totals.

front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: Milestone 2 — "I want to see the matches themselves"?
back: Round 1 is half-finished. People want to know which matches are done and what the scores were.

front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: Milestone 3 — "I want to compare predictions side by side"?
back: End of Round 1. The standings tab tells you the total, but doesn't show whose pick was wrong. You add a third screen.

front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: Milestone 4 — "Round 2 happened, the bracket shrunk"?
back: Round 1 is done. Round 2 results trickle in. You add ROUND2_MATCHES and a r2 array on every team. The whole calculateTeamScores function gets duplicated for R2:

front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: Milestone 5 — "Show me a player's tournament journey"?
back: Mid-tournament, QF in flight. The Standings + Matches + Predictions trio is the league-level view. But you want to be able to ask, "How is Wu Yize doing? Which teams backed him? Did they get points?" That's the Players tab.

front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: Milestone 6 — "I want pretty charts"?
back: Late tournament. With most matches in, you want trend visuals — points by round, accuracy by round, agreement on each match.

front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: Milestone 7 — "Wu Yize won. Crown him."?
back: Tournament ends. The Final result is in: Wu Yize 18, Murphy 17. The codebase needs to acknowledge that the champion is decided and the league is done.

front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: Putting the timeline together?
back: If you read the file with this timeline in mind, you can almost see the seams between sessions. The five *Details arrays returned by calculateTeamScores each got added in their own session. The <RoundButton> and <PicksList> components were added at slightly different times — the round button has a status prop with literal English ('FINISHED', '🏆 WU WINS 18-17'), which is unmistakably end-of-tournament wording, while <PicksList> is generic (it would render the same on day 1). The newer code is more specific to "the tournament is over;" the older code is more generic to "any tournament."

front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: Vibe prompt you would have used (the meta-pattern)?
back: There isn't a single prompt for the whole evolution — that's the point. There's one prompt per milestone, and each prompt is shaped like an addition, not a redesign:

Why is each round's logic copy-pasted (one block for r1, one for r2, …) instead of looped over ['r1', 'r2', 'qf', 'sf', 'final']? What would looping cost in readability for a vibe coder reading this file? 3. The Players tab is by far the biggest single milestone in the timeline. front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: The seven shapes of a good vibe prompt?
back: After two weeks of building this app, your prompt library should look like this:

front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: The prompt-tree of this app?
back: If you imagine the file as a tree of prompts, the trunk is Milestone 1's "Give me the standings" prompt. Every subsequent prompt is a branch off an existing branch — never the trunk. You never re-prompt the standings table from scratch. You always say "in the standings, change X." That discipline is what keeps a vibe-coded app from becoming a churning rewrite machine.

front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: Final word?
back: The thing this course was reverse-engineering wasn't just a 1,654-line file. It was the prompting strategy that grew that file without rewrites. If you internalize Lesson 4's "state up, callbacks down," Lesson 5's "extract when you copy-paste twice," Lesson 6's "data shape, not chart library," and Lesson 8's "Add X to Y showing Z," you can build the next app — pickleball ladder, World Cup pool, whatever — in maybe a third of the prompts.
